turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: In case number 16-476, Murphy v. NCAA and the consolidated case, Justice Alito has the opinion of the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: These cases concern the constitutionality of provisions of the Federal Law called the Professional and Amateur Sports Protection Act or PASPA. Among other things, PASPA makes it unlawful for a state to authorize sports gambling schemes. In 2014, the New Jersey Legislature enacted a law that had the effect of authorizing sports gambling at casinos in Atlantic City and at horse racing tracks. The major professional sports leagues and the NCAA filed suit to enjoin the implementation of this law, arguing that it violates PASPA. Defending the law, New Jersey argued that the PASPA provision prohibiting state authorization of sports gambling is unconstitutional under this Court's anti-commandeering doctrine. The United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit rejected New Jersey's argument and we now reverse. Although the term anti-commandeering sounds arcane, the anti-commandeering doctrine actually simply recognizes the fundamental feature of our Constitution's design. Under the Constitution, Congress is granted certain enumerated powers. They are considerable, but they are not unlimited and conspicuously absent from the list of powers granted to Congress is the authority to compel a state legislature either to enact or to refrain from enacting legislation. In exercising the powers given to it by the Constitution, Congress may directly regulate the activities of private parties and when the federal regulation of private conduct conflicts with state law, federal law preempt state law under the supremacy clause. But Congress lacks the authority to issue orders directly to a state legislature and that is precisely what PASPA does in prohibiting a state from authorizing sports gambling. This provision does not regulate private conduct, but instead purports to regulate state legislatures. The Constitution does not give the federal government that power. We therefore hold that the PASPA provision outlawing state authorization of sports gambling is unconstitutional. We reach a similar conclusion with respect to a provision prohibiting state licensing of sports gambling schemes. In light of our conclusion that these provisions exceed Congress' authority, we have considered whether the remaining provisions of PASPA can be severed or whether they too must fall. PASPA sets out an integrated scheme with the prohibition of state authorization and licensing at the center. Without those provisions we conclude the rest of the law cannot stand. For these reasons the decision of the Court of Appeals is reversed. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion, Justice Breyer has filed an opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part, Justice Ginsburg has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justice Breye has joined in part and Justice Sotomayor has joined in full.
